title: Multiple Exception Types
subtitle: Handle different errors in different ways
learning_objectives: Write multiple except blocks for different error types
Catch multiple exceptions in one except clause
Understand the importance of ordering except blocks correctly
Use the Exception base class as a catch-all

sections.heading: Multiple except Blocks
sections.content: One try block can have multiple except blocks — one for each type of error you want to handle differently. Python checks them in order from top to bottom and runs only the first matching one.
sections.code_output:
Result: 5.0
Error: Cannot divide by zero!
Error: Both values must be numbers!

sections.heading: Catching Multiple Exceptions at Once
sections.content: If you want to handle several exception types the same way, put them together in a tuple inside one except clause.
sections.code_output:
42
Could not parse: hello
Could not parse: None
0

sections.heading: The Exception Base Class
sections.content: All exceptions inherit from Exception. You can use except Exception as e: as a catch-all that handles any exception. Use it at the end of your except chain, after more specific handlers. This is better than a bare except: because it still lets system-level signals pass through.
sections.note: More specific except blocks must come BEFORE more general ones. If you put 'except Exception' first, it will catch everything and the specific blocks below it will never run.

exercises.title: Handle Two Error Types
exercises.prompt: Write a function lookup(data, key) that gets data[key] and converts it to an integer. Handle KeyError (print 'Key not found') and ValueError (print 'Not a number'). Test: lookup({"score": "95"}, "score"), lookup({"score": "abc"}, "score"), lookup({"score": "95"}, "name").
exercises.starter_code:
def lookup(data, key):
    # Handle KeyError and ValueError separately
    pass

print(lookup({"score": "95"}, "score"))
lookup({"score": "abc"}, "score")
lookup({"score": "95"}, "name")
hints: Put int(data[key]) in the try block
Add except KeyError: for missing keys
Add except ValueError: for non-numeric values

exercises.title: Catch Multiple at Once
exercises.prompt: Write a function safe_convert(value) that converts value to a float. If either a ValueError or TypeError occurs, return 0.0. Test: safe_convert("3.14"), safe_convert("bad"), safe_convert(None).
exercises.starter_code:
def safe_convert(value):
    # Catch ValueError and TypeError together in one except
    pass

print(safe_convert("3.14"))
print(safe_convert("bad"))
print(safe_convert(None))
hints: Use float(value) in the try block
Use except (ValueError, TypeError): to catch both at once

quiz.question: If you have multiple except blocks, which one runs?
options: The first one that matches the exception type
quiz.explanation: Python checks except blocks from top to bottom and runs the first one whose exception type matches what was raised. After that, the rest are skipped.

quiz.question: How do you catch both ValueError and TypeError with a single except clause?
quiz.explanation: Use a tuple of exception types inside parentheses: except (ValueError, TypeError):. This catches either type and runs the same handler for both.

completion_message: Nice work! You can now write sophisticated error handlers that respond differently to different problems.
